questions.text: I hold the view that my emotional steadiness allows me to be a grounding presence.
I believe that the discomfort of giving is always worth the benefit it creates for someone else.
I take the lead in negotiations and high-stakes conversations.
I am drawn to strengthening organizations that already exist.
I find that the moral pull of suffering is what keeps me honest about my priorities.
I tend to frame requests as options rather than expectations.
When I'm nervous, the sensation is so physical — dry mouth, tingling limbs — that it's impossible to ignore.
I start moving and gather resources and approvals along the way.
I notice when a group is collectively avoiding a topic that everyone is thinking about.
I find myself correcting course when I think a group is going the wrong way.
I find that the felt weight of suffering is the moral datum that I return to again and again when I need to check my reasoning.
I find that I miss when a person's body language tells a completely different story than their words.
I stick to proven methods when the stakes are high.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering applies with equal force whether the person is present or distant.
I find that directing my energy inward—toward my own improvement—is deeply satisfying.
I believe my purpose is best fulfilled when I am contributing to someone else's well-being.
I notice that I don't experience the dramatic emotional peaks and valleys that others describe.
I hold the view that orderliness in small things reflects clarity in thinking.
I rarely sense when a person is being patient with me rather than genuinely engaged.
I am energized by standing at the front of the room and guiding the discussion.
I hold the view that self-care is not indulgence—it is strategy.
I notice that when I feel vulnerable, every nerve ending seems to be exposed.
I routinely proofread, edit, or review other people's work without being asked.
I believe that resources are meant to be used in service of present opportunities.
I think that structured reasoning about distressing circumstances is a discipline that protects against the kind of impulsive action that can create new problems.
I feel that relying on willpower is exhausting; I'd rather follow my natural energy.
I track my progress methodically to ensure I'm meeting my targets.
I feel that taking a solo trip to recharge is more valuable than a group vacation.
I hold the view that accountability should be distributed across the team.
I think that rational engagement with suffering, while less dramatic than emotional engagement, tends to produce results that are more lasting and more just.
My emotional responses are proportional and consistent, which gives me a sense of reliability.
I notice when someone uses inclusive language strategically to build alliance.
I rarely detect when someone adjusts their opinion after sensing the majority view.
I believe my capacity for intense feeling is both a gift and a burden.
I hold the view that remaining emotionally even during a crisis demonstrates a kind of competence that serves everyone better.
I pick up on when a conversation is being steered toward a predetermined conclusion.
